message:
fix(headless_mux_server): cap JoinFederation peer registry + preserve first_seen_at on rejoin (ft-ry224)

## Two defects in one handler

crates/frankenterm-core/src/headless_mux_server.rs:402-416
RemoteRequest::JoinFederation blindly inserted into self.peers
with two problems:

1. **Unbounded peer-map growth (DoS).** ServerConfig has
   max_connections (for clients) and max_panes (for panes) but
   NOT max_peers. A hostile or buggy counterparty spamming
   JoinFederation with distinct node_ids grew self.peers without
   bound — eventually OOM'ing the mux server. prune_unreachable_
   peers only fires after peer_timeout_ms + an external sweep,
   so a burst is not naturally bounded. Same attack shape as
   ft-kfy99 (tmux_cc line cap), ft-phz7x (codec oversize
   tagged_len), ft-8ihuz (scrollback unbounded read).

2. **first_seen_at clobbered on rejoin.** Every JoinFederation
   fully replaced PeerInfo, including setting first_seen_at
   = epoch_ms(). The ft-lekgj recovery path tells a peer to
   re-JoinFederation after a Heartbeat sees peer_not_federated;
   every such re-join silently lost the peer's original
   federation timestamp, making 'how long has this peer been
   federated' observability lie.

## Fix

Add `max_peers: u32` to ServerConfig (default 512 — 2× the
default max_connections, well above realistic federation sizes
but low enough to bound the registry at ~128 KiB). In the
JoinFederation handler:

  - If node_id is NEW and self.peers.len() >= max_peers, return
    RemoteResponse::Error { code: "peer_registry_full" } with a
    message citing current/cap and the refused node_id.
  - If node_id already exists, preserve first_seen_at from the
    existing entry; only refresh node / status /
    last_heartbeat_at / capabilities. Re-joins don't consume a
    slot, so they're allowed even at cap — matches the "known
    peer re-joining is a heal, not a new subscriber" intuition.

New helper: `fn default_max_peers() -> u32 { 512 }`. Serde
default lets existing config files roll forward without a
migration.

## Regression tests

`join_federation_rejects_beyond_max_peers_ft_ry224`:
  - Config with max_peers=3. Join peer-0..=peer-2 successfully.
  - Peer-3 (DISTINCT, fourth) rejected with
    Error { code: "peer_registry_full" }; message must cite
    "3/3" AND the refused node_id so operators can grep the log.
  - peer_count stays at 3 — nothing consumed.
  - peer-0 re-joining (EXISTING, at cap) still succeeds —
    returns FederationJoined, peer_count still 3. Pins the
    "re-joins don't count toward the cap" contract.

`join_federation_preserves_first_seen_at_on_rejoin_ft_ry224`:
  - Join a peer, capture first_seen_at.
  - Sleep 5ms (ensure epoch_ms advances).
  - Re-join the same node_id.
  - Assert post-rejoin first_seen_at == original first_seen_at.
  - Assert post-rejoin last_heartbeat_at >= first_seen (sanity
    check that the re-join actually touched something).

## Verification

frankenterm-core --lib is still blocked on the pre-existing
unrelated compile errors (ft-kqidb / ft-uigpj / ft-s2u51 /
ft-zv3u9). This edit is purely additive: one new field on
ServerConfig (serde default makes it roll-forward compatible),
one new fn, one new conditional in the JoinFederation match
arm, plus two regression tests. No API break. Runs clean once
any of the four pre-existing blockers unblocks.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ft-fbee5","title":"pattern pack discovery followed symlinks out of tree","description":"# [HIGH] pattern-pack loader: symlink-escape via discover_packs_from_dir

**Subsystem:** patterns / scan_pipeline
**File:** crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs

## The hole

`load_pack_from_file(path, root)` routes through `sandbox_resolve` only when a
`Some(root)` is supplied. `discover_packs_from_dir`, used for
`.ft/patterns/**` discovery from a workspace root, was calling
`load_pack_from_file(path, None)` for every file + rules.toml it iterated —
so the sandbox check was **skipped** even though the dir itself was
workspace-attacker-controlled.

A hostile repo could ship:

    .ft/patterns/config.yaml -> /Users/victim/.ssh/id_rsa

`path.is_file()` follows the symlink (returns true), `load_pack_from_file`
opens the target, feeds its contents to `serde_yaml::from_str`, and on parse
failure emits:

    tracing::warn!("Skipping invalid user pack {}: {e}", path.display());

Which can echo excerpts of the target file via serde's error reporting
(`mapping expected, found: …`).

## Pre-conditions

* victim clones a hostile repo that contains a symlink under
  `.ft/patterns/`;
* frankenterm loads user packs at session start.

## Fix

`discover_packs_from_dir` now passes `Some(dir)` as the sandbox root to
`load_pack_from_file`. `sandbox_resolve` canonicalizes and rejects any
resolved path outside `dir`. Added regression test
`discover_packs_rejects_symlink_escape`.

## Residual risk

A top-level symlink replacing `.ft/patterns` itself would still escape
because the caller (`load_packs_from_config`) builds `ws_dir =
root_path.join(".ft").join("patterns")` without canonicalizing. That's a
follow-up — tracking separately.

## Sibling work

- 33e84436 security(patterns): add sandbox_resolve helper (helper introduction)
- e8397747 / 8e57c8e0 fix(limits): MAX_PACK_BYTES 16 MiB cap (orthogonal DoS layer)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:26:10.019601Z","created_by":"p3-review-audit","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:54:22.244142Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:54:22.243799Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 6ab52765 — canonicalize + in-tree check applied to pack discovery","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ft-05hfm","patterns","security"]}

 {"id":"ft-fs42k","title":"connector_outbound_bridge: dedup uses event.timestamp_ms (parallel ft-dijpe miss)","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/connector_outbound_bridge.rs:1027\` passes
\`event.timestamp_ms\` (caller-supplied via the public
\`OutboundEvent::with_timestamp_ms\` builder, line 284) to
\`check_and_record\` as the dedup eviction clock — the exact shape that
ft-dijpe just fixed in the inbound bridge.

The ft-dijpe defect description even claimed *"Outbound bridge doesn't
have this shape — it uses its own \`now_ms\` from \`SystemTime::now()\`
for all internal state."* That claim is wrong. The outbound bridge's
constructor (line 245) does use \`SystemTime::now()\` to seed
\`timestamp_ms\` by default, but:

1. The public \`with_timestamp_ms\` builder (line 284) lets any caller
   override that default to an arbitrary value.
2. \`route_event\` line 1027 then passes that caller-controlled value
   directly to \`OutboundDeduplicator::check_and_record\`, which calls
   \`evict_expired(now_ms)\` and pops anything whose
   \`now_ms - inserted_at_ms > ttl_ms\`.
3. Same replay-bypass attack as ft-dijpe: a caller submitting two
   copies of the same correlation_id with the second copy's
   \`timestamp_ms = ttl_ms + 1\` triggers eviction of the first entry
   and the replay is treated as new.

## Concrete failure path

1. Internal caller (workflow handler, retry loop, test harness) builds
   an \`OutboundEvent\` and calls \`with_timestamp_ms\` to override the
   default \`SystemTime::now()\` (legitimate reason: replaying historical
   events for backfill, fixture-driven tests, or a buggy clock-advance
   helper).
2. First dispatch with \`correlation_id=X, timestamp_ms=0\`.
3. Second dispatch with \`correlation_id=X, timestamp_ms=ttl_ms+1\`.
4. \`evict_expired\` sees \`now_ms=ttl+1 > inserted_at=0+ttl\` → pops X.
5. \`check_and_record\` finds cache empty, inserts a new X, returns
   \`true\` → second dispatch routes as if new. All downstream
   side-effects fire twice (slack post, JIRA ticket, GitHub PR
   comment, credential broker call, etc.).

Trust model is weaker than inbound's — outbound events are
internally constructed rather than attacker-supplied — but the
defense-in-depth argument applies. A buggy internal caller that
mis-sets \`timestamp_ms\` should not silently bypass dedup, and
symmetry with the inbound bridge's hardening makes the codebase
easier to reason about.

## Severity rationale

- Same shape as ft-dijpe (silent dedup bypass), one severity level
  lower because the trust model is internal-only.
- Reachable today via any internal caller misusing
  \`with_timestamp_ms\`. Future caller bugs are likelier than
  attacker reach.

## Fix sketch

Mirror the ft-dijpe fix: in \`route_event\` line 1027, compute
\`now_ms\` from \`SystemTime::now().duration_since(UNIX_EPOCH).as_millis()\`
and pass that to \`check_and_record\` instead of \`event.timestamp_ms\`.
The \`event.timestamp_ms\` field stays useful as an analytics signal
(end-to-end source→sink latency, dispatch-history correlation) but
must NOT determine when dedup entries expire.

## Regression test

\`bridge_dedup_ignores_event_timestamp_ms_for_eviction\`: dispatch X
with \`correlation_id='r', timestamp_ms=0\`, then dispatch X with
\`correlation_id='r', timestamp_ms=u64::MAX/2\`. Assert the second is
\`deduplicated=true\`. Pre-fix the second returns \`deduplicated=false\`
because evict_expired with the spoofed future-timestamp pops the live
entry.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/connector_outbound_bridge.rs\` only.
The line-1080 \`let now_ms = event.timestamp_ms\` (used downstream for
\`created_at_ms\`, \`broker_checked_at_ms\`, \`dispatched_at_ms\`) is a
separate concern — those telemetry timestamps arguably should also
be SystemTime::now() but that's a wider change. This bead covers
ONLY the dedup eviction clock.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:25:28.629996Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:26:19.140282Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:26:19.139925Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of ft-ptjlp (same defect, same line, parallel filing race). Sibling agent already applied the SystemTime::now() fix in working tree at connector_outbound_bridge.rs:1037-1044 with their own ft-ptjlp tag in the inline comment. Closing this bead so ft-ptjlp owns the fix and the test work. Reviewed their fix — it's identical to what I would have written (SystemTime::now() → dedup_now_ms → check_and_record), correctly preserves event.timestamp_ms for downstream telemetry uses.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-gofz5","title":"[EPIC] Release engineering: first tagged release, cargo install validation, and binary distribution pipeline","description":"# Goal
Establish a working release pipeline so that \`cargo install --git ... ft\` actually works, release binaries are available for major platforms, and there are tagged versions users can reference.

# Background
The reality check found:
- Only 1 git tag exists ("backup-before-rewrite") — no version releases
- \`cargo install\` is advertised in README Quick Install but never validated
- 6 GitHub Actions workflows exist (ci.yml, release.yml, etc.) but release.yml has never been triggered for a real release
- The project is pre-1.0 with 4,288 commits but zero releases
- Build is resource-intensive (~793k LOC core library) which may cause cargo install to fail on memory-constrained machines

# Why This Matters
A project that can't be installed by its advertised method has zero users. Even for internal/swarm use, tagged releases let agents pin to known-good versions. Release hygiene signals project maturity.

# Architectural Intent
1. Validate that \`cargo install --git ... ft\` succeeds on a clean machine (Linux x86_64, macOS arm64 at minimum).
2. Create v0.1.0 tag with honest release notes reflecting current capabilities.
3. Ensure release.yml workflow produces downloadable binaries.
4. Add a CI job that runs \`cargo install\` from git to catch install regressions.

# Acceptance Criteria
1. \`cargo install --git https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/frankenterm.git ft\` succeeds on Linux x86_64 and macOS arm64.
2. A v0.1.0 tag exists with release notes that honestly describe working features, partial features, and known limitations.
3. release.yml produces downloadable binaries for at least Linux x86_64 and macOS arm64.
4. CI includes a cargo install smoke test that runs on every push to main.
5. README Quick Install section includes minimum system requirements (RAM, disk, Rust nightly version).","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-04-06T18:55:35.783442Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-11T02:07:18.344985Z","closed_at":"2026-04-11T02:07:18.344593Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ci","distribution","goal-line","release"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-gofz5","depends_on_id":"ft-d0ez0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-06T18:59:00.127254Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"ft-gofz5.1","title":"Validate cargo install from git on clean Linux x86_64 and macOS arm64 environments","description":"# Goal
Prove that the advertised Quick Install command actually works on fresh machines.

# Design
1. On a clean Linux x86_64 environment (CI runner or fresh container):
   - Install Rust nightly (matching rust-toolchain.toml)
   - Run: cargo install --git https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/frankenterm.git ft
   - Verify: ft --version produces output
   - Verify: ft doctor runs without crash
   - Record: peak RAM usage, disk usage, wall time

2. On macOS arm64 (CI runner or local):
   - Same steps as above
   - Additional: verify LLVM/clang dependency resolution (cc alias issue documented in MEMORY.md)

3. If install fails, diagnose and fix:
   - Likely issues: memory exhaustion (793k LOC), missing system deps, cc alias conflict
   - Fix in Cargo.toml, build.rs, or CI configuration
   - Document minimum requirements

# Acceptance Criteria
1. cargo install succeeds on both platforms with exit code 0.
2. ft --version and ft doctor both work post-install.
3. Peak RAM and disk requirements documented.
4.  {"id":"ft-grz8r","title":"[EPIC] Eliminate verified first-party placeholders and stubs","description":"# Goal
Eliminate every verified first-party placeholder, stub, mock-like runtime branch, and misleading partial implementation surfaced by the 2026-04-02 audit.

Every in-scope item must end in exactly one of two states:
1. Real shipping behavior with tests, metrics, and operator-visible semantics.
2. Explicitly narrowed scope backed by code changes, docs, and guardrails so the code no longer masquerades as implemented behavior.

# Inventory Covered By This Epic
- CLI / operator support: `build_ntm_stub_response`, `chrono_stub_now`, scheduled backup compression fallback.
- Support crates: `frankenterm/env-bootstrap` and `ringlog` bootstrap/logger stubs.
- Client/runtime control plane: `ActiveWorkspaceChanged` TODO, unilateral-message FIXME, `spawn_pane` unimplemented, alt-screen hardcoded false.
- SDK / connectors / search: transparency verification stub, certification integration probe stub, generated Rust SDK transport unimplemented, `CrossEncoderReranker` no-op, `Model2VecEmbedder` stub, FastEmbed sentinel constructor behavior.
- Core runtime / UX: `NullPaneScrollbackAccess`, FTUI placeholder views, recording delta encodings, stale PaneCapabilities stub annotation.
- Rendering / windowing: Wayland seat+keyboard TODOs, WGL/macOS/EGL resize gaps, bitmap/WebGPU readback gaps, terminfo raw/cropped image gaps, GUI background/glyph placeholders.
- Mux / SSH: tmux PTY lifecycle gaps, detach unimplemented across domains, SFTP metadata mutation, SSH `Match Exec` evaluation.

# Scope Boundary
Only first-party source is in scope. Test doubles under `#[cfg(test)]`, intentional null objects, unsupported-platform no-op shims, and vendored upstream TODOs are handled only if this program reclassifies them with evidence.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-04-03T00:26:38.047585Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-11T02:31:29.254960Z","closed_at":"2026-04-11T02:31:29.253673Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["placeholder-remediation","stub-audit"]}
 {"id":"ft-gy9eo","title":"codec::Pdu::stream_decode OOMs on oversized varbincode container lengths","description":"Fuzz target `pdu_stream_decode` found an out-of-memory bug in `codec::Pdu::stream_decode` when varbincode deserialization trusted an attacker-controlled container length and preallocated a huge sequence.

Repro command:
`CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-pane4-target-fuzz-codec-dev cargo fuzz run --dev pdu_stream_decode --features codec-fuzz-targets -- artifact /Users/jemanuel/projects/frankenterm/fuzz/artifacts/pdu_stream_decode/oom-5c320deb87390600368019c6ffe451a1e575b82b`

Crasher artifact: `/Users/jemanuel/projects/frankenterm/fuzz/artifacts/pdu_stream_decode/oom-5c320deb87390600368019c6ffe451a1e575b82b`
Base64: `DkQEAAQA/////3FxcTBxcf4=`
Hex: `0E 44 04 00 04 00 FF FF FF FF 71 71 71 30 71 71 FE`

Expected behavior: reject the frame before allocation.

# Design

1. Run: cargo test -p frankenterm-core --lib mcp 2>&1 | tee /tmp/mcp_errors.log

   - Type mismatches from recent module changes -> update test expectations
   - Missing types/imports due to feature gates -> add #[cfg(feature)] or fix import paths
   - Async runtime errors (#[tokio::test] vs asupersync) -> migrate to sync or runtime_compat
   - Stale test data referencing old API shapes -> update fixtures
3. Fix each error, verifying after each fix that the change compiles
4. Final run: cargo test -p frankenterm-core --lib mcp -- all 170 tests green

## Common failure patterns to watch for
- MCP submodules use #[path] declarations — if a submodule file moves or renames, the path attribute breaks

- robot_types.rs changes may have updated MCP response envelope shapes without updating mcp.rs test expectations

- Diagnostic log: mcp_test_fix_<timestamp>.log with each error, category, fix applied
- Before/after test counts to verify no tests lost

1. cargo test -p frankenterm-core --lib mcp passes with 0 failures under default features.
2. All 170 test functions execute (none skipped, none #[ignore]d without documented reason).
3. No semantic changes to test intent — only update expectations to match current API.
4. Diagnostic log documenting each fix for future reference.
5.  {"id":"ft-n3mim","title":"[mor:ergonomics] Error::Runtime(String) is the '3am diagnostic dead-end' variant","description":"# Observation

Memory and grep confirm the frankenterm-core Error enum has no
\`Generic\` variant — the convention is \`Runtime(String)\` for
ad-hoc failures. Every time a caller needed a fresh error shape and
didn't want to define a typed variant, they reached for
\`Error::Runtime\`.

At 3am during an incident, the operator sees:

    Error: runtime: mux pool write failed: io error: Broken pipe (os error 32)

This is a String wrapped in an enum variant. You can't match on it,
you can't branch on it programmatically, you can't build a
dashboard row off it. The information that would make the incident
tractable — is this a transient network blip, a crashed mux, a
kernel panic, a rate limit, a disk-full? — is buried in a
string the operator has to grep for.

# Question

For each subsystem that currently stuffs into \`Error::Runtime(...)\`,
is there a typed variant that would make triage faster? Specific
candidates worth auditing:

- \`runtime_compat\` cancel / timeout wrappers — currently stringify
  the underlying failure into Runtime
- \`mux_pool\` write/read failures that aren't already covered by
  \`WeztermError\`
- \`asupersync\` lifecycle errors the caller needs to distinguish
  from transient I/O

A principle: every error the operator would act on differently
deserves a typed variant. Everything else can stay generic.

# 3am impact

This is the class of issue that doesn't show up in normal workflow.
It only bites when production is on fire. The cost is invisible
until the cost is enormous. The fix is incremental: every Runtime
creation site is a candidate for typing, and typing them one-at-a-
time pays immediately in the next incident that touches that
subsystem.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"question","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:36:27.030250Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:52:57.270374Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:52:57.270015Z","close_reason":"implemented","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["3am","ergonomics","error-types","mor"],"comments":[{"id":3600,"issue_id":"ft-n3mim","author":"jemanuel","text":"Commit 79702a50 introduces typed top-level runtime/pane/watchdog error variants plus source enums in crates/frankenterm-core/src/error.rs, deprecates Error::Runtime as a last-resort catch-all, and migrates clean operator-facing call-sites in crates/frankenterm-core/src/export.rs, crates/frankenterm-core/src/search_explain.rs, and crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs away from stringly Runtime(...). Added regression coverage for typed pane/watchdog/runtime classification and mock WezTerm error context. Local verification: rustfmt + git diff --check passed on the touched files. Requested rch lane (structured_error_ test prefix) is still in full-workspace sync on worker vmi1227854 with no compile/test result line yet. Note: crates/frankenterm-core/src/runtime.rs still has unrelated live edits in this checkout, so I intentionally did not widen this bead into that file.","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:52:57Z"}]}

 {"id":"ft-nk6u9","title":"[MEDIUM] simd_scan ansi_byte_count misses OSC/DCS/PM/APC string bodies (terminates on first letter after ESC)","description":"# Defect

## Concrete failure path

`scan_newlines_and_ansi_*` (4 variants in `crates/frankenterm-core/src/simd_scan.rs`) treats any ASCII letter (0x40..=0x7E, excluding `[`) immediately after ESC as the terminator of the escape sequence. That rule is correct for CSI (`ESC [ … letter`) and for single-char C1 controls like `ESC M` (reverse index). It is **wrong** for the five C1 introducers that open a *string* sequence:

| Intro byte | Sequence | Continues until |
|------------|----------|-----------------|
| `]` (0x5D) | OSC (Operating System Command — window titles, clipboard, hyperlinks) | ST (`ESC \\`) or BEL (0x07) |
| `P` (0x50) | DCS (Device Control String — e.g., Sixel graphics, DECRQSS replies) | ST |
| `^` (0x5E) | PM (Privacy Message) | ST |
| `_` (0x5F) | APC (Application Program Command — e.g., Kitty graphics) | ST |
| `X` (0x58) | SOS (Start Of String) | ST |

All five land in the `0x40..=0x7E && != '['` branch and prematurely exit the escape. The string body + ST/BEL bytes are then counted as **plain text**, not ANSI.

## Reproducer (currently NO test covers this)

```rust
let data = b"\x1b]0;window title\x07after";
let scan = scan_newlines_and_ansi(data);
// Current:  ansi_byte_count = 2  (just ESC + ])
// Correct:  ansi_byte_count = 19 (ESC + ] + "0;window title" + BEL)
assert_eq!(scan.ansi_byte_count, 19);
```

Same bug in the `_with_state` variants — the cross-chunk state carries only `in_escape: bool` with no notion of "string mode," so the first chunk's body bytes leak into the "non-ANSI" count.

## Blast radius

- `ansi_density` (`OutputScanMetrics::ansi_density`) underreports on panes that emit OSC/DCS/PM/APC/SOS sequences — common in production: window-title updates from shell prompts, terminfo hyperlinks, Sixel graphics, Kitty image protocol, DCS replies to DECRQSS probes.
- `ScanMetricsSummary` is consumed by compression and backpressure decision paths; an underreport makes the system think a pane is producing plain text when it is actually producing high-ANSI output.
- Not a crash, not data loss — silent wrong answer in the metrics.

## Fix plan

1. Extend `OutputScanState` with a `StringPhase` — `InString` and `InStringSawEsc` so the stateful scanner can resume a partial `ESC \\` terminator across chunk boundaries.
2. After ESC is seen, if the next byte is one of \{0x50, 0x58, 0x5D, 0x5E, 0x5F\}, transition to `InString` instead of clearing `in_escape`.
3. While in `InString`: count every byte as ANSI; terminate on BEL (0x07) or ESC \\ (0x1b 0x5c).
4. Patch all four scan functions (memchr + scalar, stateful + non-stateful).
5. Add targeted regression tests: basic OSC, OSC + ST, OSC + BEL, DCS, PM, APC, chunk split through the body, chunk split on ESC-\\, and OSC containing embedded newlines (verify newline_count is unaffected).

+{"id":"ft-s4myu","title":"storage: foreign_keys PRAGMA not applied on connection open for up-to-date DBs — CASCADE deletes silently skip","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs` `StorageHandle::with_config` (line 6002) opens the writer connection via `Connection::open(&db_path_owned)` and then calls `initialize_schema(&conn)`. The `PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON` statement is ONLY present inside `SCHEMA_SQL` (line 104) which is executed via `conn.execute_batch(SCHEMA_SQL)` at line 4349/4375.

For an UP-TO-DATE database (user_version == SCHEMA_VERSION, the common case on every frankenterm restart), `initialize_schema` short-circuits at line 4341-4344:

```rust
if current == SCHEMA_VERSION {
    // Already up to date
    ensure_ft_meta(conn, SCHEMA_VERSION)?;
    return Ok(());
}
```

...returning WITHOUT executing SCHEMA_SQL. SQLite's `PRAGMA foreign_keys` is PER-CONNECTION — it must be re-issued on every new connection. Since the ONLY place that sets it is the schema-init path, every writer connection on an existing DB has `foreign_keys = OFF` (the SQLite default).

## Impact (HIGH)

Every ON DELETE CASCADE in the schema silently fails to fire. Concrete example:

```rust
fn prune_session_checkpoints_sync(conn, session_id, retention) -> Result<usize> {
    conn.execute("DELETE FROM session_checkpoints WHERE session_id = ?1 AND id NOT IN (...)")
}
```

`mux_pane_state` rows reference `session_checkpoints(id) ON DELETE CASCADE` at schema line 646. On an up-to-date DB, the DELETE removes the checkpoint row but leaves ORPHAN mux_pane_state rows. Over time these accumulate indefinitely.

Same pattern affects 15+ other references in SCHEMA_SQL (output_segments→panes, events→panes, workflow_executions→panes, etc.). Every `delete_session`, retention sweep, and explicit pane cleanup path leaks orphan rows.

Silent data corruption accumulates across restarts. A fresh-DB test suite (which runs SCHEMA_SQL) would never catch this.

## Fix

Apply `PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON` unconditionally on every connection open, after `Connection::open` and before the initialize_schema short-circuit. One-liner:

```rust
let conn = Connection::open(&db_path_owned)?;
check_and_recover_wal(&conn, &db_path_owned)?;
conn.pragma_update(None, "foreign_keys", true)
    .map_err(|e| StorageError::Database(format!("Failed to enable foreign keys: {e}")))?;
initialize_schema(&conn)?;
```

This is idempotent (the pragma is a per-connection toggle) and costs O(1) per open.

## Tests

- `prune_session_checkpoints_cascades_mux_pane_state`: open storage on pre-populated DB, call prune with retention=0, assert every mux_pane_state row for deleted checkpoints is gone (vs current behavior where they persist).
- `fresh_db_and_existing_db_both_enforce_foreign_keys`: open on fresh + reopen on existing, in each case attempt to INSERT a mux_pane_state with a non-existent checkpoint_id, assert SQLITE_CONSTRAINT_FOREIGNKEY.

## Severity

HIGH. Silent data corruption, invisible to fresh-DB tests, accumulates across restarts. 15+ FK definitions in SCHEMA_SQL silently no-op for existing databases. Fix is one line.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:35:18.699462Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:38:37.071424Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-s6l5b","title":"[testing-fuzzing] pin match_rule_glob invariants under proptest (empty patterns, star-wildcards, length preservation)","description":"# Gap

events.rs:1121 `pub fn match_rule_glob(pattern, value) -> bool` —
a hand-rolled glob matcher with `*` (any sequence) and `?` (any
single char) support. 19 inline example tests at events.rs:2827+ cover
specific cases. No proptest / property-based coverage exists.

# Risk

Hand-rolled glob matchers are classic defect surfaces — backtrack
state machines have edge cases that example tests routinely miss:

1. Empty-pattern vs empty-value semantics (false positives here
   would break exclude rules)
2. Pattern of N `?`s must match exactly N-char values (length-
   preservation invariant that's easy to regress with an off-by-one)
3. `"*"` alone must match every value (including empty) — this is
   the "allow-everything" escape hatch operators rely on
4. Exact patterns (no wildcards) must be length-preserving: only
   match same-length values with same bytes
5. Idempotence: `match_rule_glob(x, x) == true` for any exact pattern x

# Fix

New proptest suite in events.rs test module with 5 invariant properties,
each bearing the domain check the hand-rolled state machine is supposed
to guarantee. Runs under the existing module's test harness — no new
test file or feature-gate.

# Catches

- Backtrack-reset off-by-one at events.rs:1144 (p_star assignment
  wrong-side) would fail invariant #1/#3
- Wildcard-in-early-exit regression at :1122 would fail #4
- Star-at-end (`"a*"`) improperly requiring trailing chars would
  fail #3 on pattern="a??" value="abc"

Shipping as a net-positive guard, not a known-bug fix.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:23:36.694100Z","created_by":"pane3-glob-proptest","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:29:58.443230Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:29:58.442992Z","close_reason":"Landed in f3e3f68c (test(events): add proptest coverage for match_rule_glob invariants).  {"id":"ft-trby3","title":"[reality-gap] RecorderBackendKind::FrankenSqlite is enum-reachable but runtime-unreachable","description":"# Claim (README)

README.md:62 lists "**Replay & Forensics**: Capture, replay, and
diff decision graphs for post-incident analysis and regression
testing" as a core feature. No distinction between backends is
offered — the user's mental model is "replay just works."

# Reality (code)

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/recorder_storage.rs:560-578`:

```rust
match config.backend {
    RecorderBackendKind::AppendLog => { ...opens storage... }
    RecorderBackendKind::FrankenSqlite => Err(
        RecorderStorageError::BackendUnavailable {
            backend: RecorderBackendKind::FrankenSqlite,
            message: "frankensqlite backend not yet implemented".to_string(),
        }
    ),
}
```

The enum variant is public, serde-deserializable (confirmed by
presence on `RecorderStorageConfig.backend` field), and reachable
from any operator who writes `backend = "frankensqlite"` (or
whatever serde rename applies) in their ft.toml's recorder section.

# Failure shape

Operator configures frankensqlite → app bootstraps → recorder
bootstrap returns `BackendUnavailable` → app either:
  (a) aborts with the "not yet implemented" message (informative);
  (b) silently falls back to AppendLog (if caller does);
  (c) propagates the error and the whole watch daemon fails to start.

Which branch fires depends on callers of `bootstrap_recorder_storage`
— worth verifying in a follow-up. Best case (a) wastes operator's
time; worst case (c) is a surprise crash.

# Recommended disposition

Either:
  (a) Remove `FrankenSqlite` from the serde-exposed enum until the
      backend is actually implemented. Zero blast radius — the
      variant is only reachable by operator opt-in today.
  (b) Keep the variant but add a deprecation/preview flag that
      operators must explicitly set (e.g., `experimental_backend =
      true`) so accidental selection is impossible.
  (c) Implement the FrankenSqlite backend.

(a) or (b) ship in minutes; (c) is a subsystem-sized undertaking.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"docs","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-21T20:44:30.571102Z","created_by":"reality-check","updated_at":"2026-04-21T20:51:20.515092Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T20:51:20.514877Z","close_reason":"README + flight-recorder docs now disclose that append_log is the only shipped recorder backend and frankensqlite remains rollout/test-only until bootstrap support lands.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["reality-gap","recorder","storage"]}
 {"id":"ft-trot7","title":"telemetry::Histogram::record poisons total_sum/min/max on f64::INFINITY (NaN guard is too narrow)","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/telemetry.rs:339\` — \`Histogram::record\`
guards against NaN but does **not** guard against \`f64::INFINITY\` or
\`f64::NEG_INFINITY\`. The recently-added [ft-b4l62] guard catches NaN
because NaN poisons \`partial_cmp\` and the running sum, but Inf has the
same poisoning shape and slips through unflagged.

## Concrete failure path

1. Caller invokes \`Histogram::record(f64::INFINITY)\`. Easy upstream
   trigger: any latency calculation of the form
   \`numerator / denom_seconds\` where \`denom_seconds == 0.0\` (clock
   skew, mocked time fixture, division-before-clamp bug). Or a
   subtraction of two timestamps that overflowed to Inf in a longer
   pipeline.
2. Line 340 \`if value.is_nan() { return; }\` — Inf is not NaN, falls
   through to mutate state.
3. Line 344 \`self.total_sum += value\`. \`f64 + INF = INF\`. \`total_sum\`
   is now \`INF\` permanently — every subsequent finite sample preserves
   the INF (\`INF + finite = INF\`).
4. Line 348-349 \`if value > self.max { self.max = value }\`. Initial
   \`max = NEG_INFINITY\` so \`INF > NEG_INFINITY\` is true → \`max = INF\`.
   Every later finite value compares \`finite > INF\` → false, so \`max\`
   stays at INF forever.
5. \`Histogram::mean()\` (line 401) returns \`INF / count = INF\`.
6. \`Histogram::summary()\` returns \`Some(INF)\` for \`mean\`, \`max\`, and
   potentially \`p99\`. \`HistogramSummary\` is \`Serialize\`; serde_json's
   default serializer emits \`null\` for non-finite f64 (RFC 8259 forbids
   Inf in JSON), silently dropping the value from telemetry payloads
   that downstream dashboards consume.
7. Symmetric for \`record(f64::NEG_INFINITY)\` — \`total_sum\` and \`min\`
   become NEG_INFINITY permanently, mean reports \`-INF\`.

## Severity rationale

- Same poisoning shape as ft-b4l62 (NaN), ft-761tz (NaN in
  disk_pressure), ft-yskcu (NaN in continuous_backpressure), ft-7s0vw
  (overflow in event_id) — silent wrong answer with no self-recovery.
- Existing NaN guard makes the absence of an Inf guard surprising:
  reviewers reading the code see \`is_nan()\` and assume the histogram
  is hardened, when it is not.
- Reachable via legitimate division-by-zero in any client computing
  durations or rates as f64.

## Fix sketch

1. \`Histogram::record\` (line 340) — broaden the guard from
   \`if value.is_nan()\` to \`if !value.is_finite()\`. \`f64::is_finite\`
   is true iff the value is neither NaN nor ±Infinity.
2. Update the doc comment and \`[ft-b4l62]\` reference to note that the
   guard now covers Inf as well.
3. Regression tests covering: (a) record(INFINITY) is no-op,
   total_sum stays 0.0, mean stays None; (b) record(NEG_INFINITY) is
   no-op; (c) record(finite) then record(INFINITY) then record(finite)
   — second finite blends correctly without Inf contamination;
   (d) summary().max stays Some(finite_value) after an Inf attempt.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/telemetry.rs\` only. No cross-crate API
changes. \`HistogramSummary\` shape unchanged.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:58:02.402142Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T12:06:14.413298Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T12:06:14.413032Z","close_reason":"Fixed in commit f73e139a: Histogram::record now uses !value.is_finite() to reject both NaN and ±INFINITY. 4 regression tests added covering pos-Inf, neg-Inf, all-Inf, and summary().max stays finite after Inf attempt. All 74 histogram-related tests pass against /tmp/ft-rv-p0-target. Pattern matches the arithmetic-poison family alongside ft-b4l62 (NaN guard, original), ft-761tz, ft-yskcu, ft-7s0vw.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

+{"id":"ft-tzwuw","title":"mcp_tools ca.search/ca.view/ca.status accept timeout_secs=0, fire immediate-timeout","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs\` cass tools (ca.search line
590, ca.view line 683, ca.status line 763) all pass
\`params.timeout_secs\` directly to \`cass_client_with_timeout()\` without
validating that it's >= 1.

The JSON-Schema declarations for these tools advertise
\`"timeout_secs": { "type": "integer", "minimum": 1, "maximum": 600,
"default": 15 }\` (lines 526, 636, 726), but \`serde_json::from_value\`
does not enforce JSON-Schema bounds — it happily deserializes \`0\` as
a valid u64.

## Concrete failure path

1. MCP client (or any caller that doesn't pre-validate against the
   schema) sends \`{"query": "x", "timeout_secs": 0}\` to ca.search.
2. \`serde_json::from_value\` accepts the 0 → \`params.timeout_secs = 0\`.
3. \`cass_client_with_timeout(0)\` constructs a CassClient with
   \`timeout = Duration::from_secs(0)\` (cass.rs:781-784).
4. \`run_with_cx\` (cass.rs:961) calls
   \`timeout_with_cx(cx, Duration::from_secs(0), cmd.output()).await\`
   — a zero-duration timeout fires immediately, before \`cmd.output()\`
   has any chance to execute.
5. Returns \`CassError::Timeout { timeout_secs: 0 }\` to the MCP client
   on EVERY call. The client sees a confusing "cass timeout (0 secs)"
   error and may not realize the issue is its own \`timeout_secs: 0\`
   parameter rather than a slow cass binary.

Same defect shape as ft-t62hq (already fixed for wa.wait_for and
wa.send tools at lines 1149, 1873) — three sibling cass tools were
missed in that sweep.

## Severity rationale

- Silent UX failure: caller's request always times out without ever
  invoking the underlying tool.
- Reachable via any client that doesn't validate against the
  advertised schema (in practice: most language SDKs that just pass
  through user-supplied JSON, plus any test fixture that omits
  timeout_secs default-checking).
- Not a crash, not data corruption.

## Fix sketch

Mirror the ft-t62hq guard at the top of each \`call(\)\` for the three
cass tools:

\`\`\`rust
if params.timeout_secs == 0 {
    let envelope = McpEnvelope::<()>::error(
        MCP_ERR_INVALID_ARGS,
        "timeout_secs must be >= 1 (got 0)".to_string(),
        Some(
            "The ca.<tool> schema declares timeout_secs with \\
             minimum: 1; omit the field to use the default (15)."
                .to_string(),
        ),
        elapsed_ms(start),
    );
    return envelope_to_content(envelope);
}
\`\`\`

## Regression tests

Three integration-style tests at the params layer:
- \`ca_search_rejects_zero_timeout_secs\`
- \`ca_view_rejects_zero_timeout_secs\`
- \`ca_status_rejects_zero_timeout_secs\`

Each invokes the tool with \`timeout_secs: 0\` and asserts the response
is an Error envelope with \`code = MCP_ERR_INVALID_ARGS\` and the
"timeout_secs must be >= 1" message — proving the guard fires before
\`cass_client_with_timeout\` is ever called.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs\` only. No schema changes —
the schemas already declare \`minimum: 1\`. No changes to \`cass.rs\` —
\`with_timeout_secs(0)\` is a documented "instant timeout" primitive
that other callers may legitimately want.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:39:11.699057Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:39:17.818198Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-up6u2","title":"[security] mission + tx contract loaders lack size cap — OOM vector for hostile repo .ft/mission/*.json","description":"# Bug

`mcp_load_mission_from_path` (mcp_missions.rs:257) and
`mcp_load_mission_tx_contract_from_path` (mcp_missions.rs:141) both
use bare `std::fs::read_to_string(path)` with no stat-before-read
size guard.

Pattern-pack loader has this exact guard (ft-05hfm, MAX_PACK_BYTES =
16 MiB). Mission files under `.ft/mission/*.json` have the same
trust property as patterns under `.ft/patterns/*.{yaml,json,toml}` —
a hostile repo can ship them, and they're loaded at MCP tool
invocation time.

# Attack

1. Hostile repo ships `.ft/mission/active.json` = 5 GiB of garbage.
2. Operator (or agent via wa.mission_state MCP) opens the project.
3. `wa.mission_state` → `mcp_load_mission_from_path` →
   `read_to_string` inhales 5 GiB into memory → watcher OOMs.

Same attack for `.ft/mission/tx-active.json` via wa.tx_show / wa.tx_run.

`resolve_workspace_scoped_path` (mcp_missions.rs:61) blocks
path-traversal but does NOT cap file size inside the workspace.

# Fix

Add `MAX_MISSION_BYTES` + `MAX_TX_CONTRACT_BYTES` constants (16 MiB
each, matching MAX_PACK_BYTES) with a stat-before-read check + a
second-line check after read, mirroring the pattern-pack loader
structure.

Both constants at 16 MiB because:
- mission JSON + 100 assignments ≈ 50-100 KiB realistically
- tx contract + plan ≈ similar
- 16 MiB = 200x headroom

# Tests

Regression tests similar to `load_pack_from_file_rejects_oversize_yaml`:
  - `mcp_load_mission_from_path_rejects_oversize_json`
  - `mcp_load_mission_tx_contract_from_path_rejects_oversize_json`","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:25:19.149935Z","created_by":"pane3-mission-cap","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:45:21.408462Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:45:21.408112Z","close_reason":"Landed in 3e2ae80c. Preserved historical robot.tx_not_found / robot.mission_not_found error codes on NotFound branch.  {"id":"ft-xgs7n","title":"[testing-fuzzing] pin extract_delta overlap-existence invariant: previous.ends_with(current[..len-delta.len()])","description":"# Gap

proptest_ingest_extract_delta_fuzz.rs covers 5 MRs (crash-freedom,
identity, content-suffix, pure-append, determinism) + 2 inline
regression tests, but does NOT pin the primary semantic contract of
the algorithm: **when Content(delta) is returned, the non-delta head
of `current` must be a tail of `previous`**. Formally:

    extract_delta(previous, current, _) = Content(delta)
    ⇒ previous.ends_with(&current[..current.len() - delta.len()])

This is the invariant the sliding-window search ACTUALLY proves
(search_window[pos..] == current[..overlap_len], where
search_window = previous[search_start..], so previous ends with
current[..overlap_len]). Without it, the algorithm could return a
Content(delta) where the "non-delta prefix" is pure fabrication —
the existing MR3 suffix-check proves current ends with delta but
says nothing about the overlap being real.

Adding this test catches any regression in the overlap-candidate
acceptance logic (`search_window[pos..] == current[..overlap_len]`
on ingest.rs:1711) that accidentally accepts a non-matching window.

Fix: new proptest in proptest_ingest_extract_delta_fuzz.rs covering
every path (pure-append fast path + sliding-window search).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:04:40.819353Z","created_by":"pane3-ingest-mr","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:13:32.759851Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:13:32.759521Z","close_reason":"Landed in 5eb9f956 (fix(apc, backpressure, ingest): …, overlap-existence proptest) — extract_delta_content_overlap_exists_in_previous added to tests/proptest_ingest_extract_delta_fuzz.rs:207.  {"id":"ft-xv561","title":"[HIGH] pattern-engine regex construction has no backtrack limit — workspace-local ReDoS via user packs","description":"Found during /security-audit-for-saas pass #2 (pane 5, 2026-04-21).

## Summary
The pattern engine compiles user-supplied regex patterns via
`fancy_regex::Regex::new(raw)` with NO backtrack limit or match
timeout. An attacker who can drop a YAML/JSON pattern pack into
`~/.config/wa/patterns/` or a repo's `.ft/patterns/` directory can
ship a catastrophic-backtracking regex that hangs `ft watch` the
first time matching pane output triggers the pattern's anchor.

## Evidence
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs:13
  `use fancy_regex::Regex;`
- patterns.rs:512 (RuleDef::validate_regex)
  `Regex::new(regex).map_err(...)` — no `.backtrack_limit(...)`.
- patterns.rs:780 (build_engine_index)
  `let regex = Regex::new(raw).map_err(...)` — same.
- patterns.rs:920 (load_packs_from_config) + :935
  (load_pack_from_id) — pack files are loaded from
  `config.resolved_user_packs_dir()` (default
  `~/.config/wa/patterns/`) AND from `.ft/patterns/` inside the
  resolved workspace root. The latter is an attacker-controlled
  directory when a repo is cloned from an untrusted source.
- mcp.rs:2010 (pattern-engine matching hot path)
  invokes `regex.captures_iter(text)` on every segment for every
  rule whose anchor matched. A single-threaded ReDoS blocks the
  matching worker; fancy_regex's default backtracking engine has
  no internal budget.

## Attack vector
1. attacker commits `.ft/patterns/evil.json` with a rule like
   ```json
   {
     "id": "evil.dos",
     "agent_type": "wezterm",
     "event_type": "noop",
     "anchors": ["a"],
     "regex": "^(a+)+$"
   }
   ```
2. user clones repo and runs `ft watch`.
3. the first pane output containing `"aaa...aaab"` (any near-match
   of the anchor) triggers captures_iter, which enters catastrophic
   backtracking. The pattern worker hangs indefinitely, wedging the
   detection pipeline.

No code execution — DoS only. But `ft watch` is the primary
operator surface; wedging the detection loop silently destroys
the "intelligent detection" claim the README makes.

## Fix direction
Two layers:

1. Compilation-time gate (quick):
   - Replace `Regex::new(raw)` with `fancy_regex::RegexBuilder::new(raw).backtrack_limit(1_000_000).build()`
     at patterns.rs:512 and :780. 1M steps is plenty for any
     legitimate rule and caps worst-case match cost.
   - Propagate the resulting `fancy_regex::Error::RuntimeError` from
     `captures_iter` as a `Detection` of kind `RegexRuntimeError`
     instead of hanging.

2. Match-time budget (robust):
   - Wrap each `regex.captures_iter(text)` call in a time-bounded
     harness (e.g., spawn a short-lived asupersync task with a
     100ms timeout). On timeout, quarantine the offending rule and
     surface a "rule disabled due to catastrophic match cost"
     telemetry event so operators can triage.

## Severity rationale
- HIGH (P1): attacker-controlled repo → DoS on user's machine.
- Not CRITICAL: local exploitation only (repo-clone vector), no
  network reach, no data exfil, no code execution.

## Out of scope for this bead
- RE2/Hyperscan migration (much larger rework).
- Per-rule memory budget (separate concern).

## Related
- ft-security-mcp-path-traversal (30988e53) — same audit pass
  already fixed the MCP arbitrary-file-read vector.

Filed per /security-audit-for-saas pass #2; recommend shipping the
compilation-time gate within one follow-up session.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T04:04:22.269417Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T04:14:08.976852Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T04:14:08.976108Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 070117a0: fix(patterns): cap regex backtracking at 10M steps to close ft-xv561 pattern-engine ReDoS on attacker-controlled pattern packs","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","patterns","redos","security"]}
